core_intuitions: A naive agent re-sends its whole growing prefix on every turn, so the tokens billed are the triangular AREA under the growth line (≈ ½ a N², quadratic in turns); capping the context at C flattens that area to O(N·C).

The EFFECTIVE window is smaller than the ADVERTISED window: accuracy is U-shaped in position (lost-in-the-middle) and decays as length and distractors grow (context rot), so the headline token number is a capacity, not a recommendation.

Attention has edge advantages (primacy/attention-sink at the start, recency at the end) and a dead zone in the middle — so the highest-value content must sit at the window's start or very end, never buried mid-context.
The window is scarce, volatile RAM to be managed like a memory allocator: give each segment a budget, PIN the load-bearing pieces, and escalate on overflow in cost order evict → truncate → compact.

Durable state belongs on 'disk' (a PLAN.md / external store) that is paged into the window each turn: it survives compaction and can be rebuilt from ground truth rather than from lossy, compounding summaries.

Prefix caching reuses the prior KV cache only up to the first byte that differs, so ordering context most-stable → least-stable (and never mutating an early segment) turns the repeated prefix into a ~10× discount; compaction rewrites the head and busts the cache.

new_figures.name: quadratic-cost-of-accumulation
new_figures.concept: Why naive context accumulation bills O(N²) tokens — the cost is the triangular AREA of re-sent prefixes — and how a context cap flattens it to O(N·C).
new_figures.rationale: This is the single quantitative claim the chapter calls 'the reason every serious agent harness has a context-management strategy.' The equation T_in = Σ L_t is abstract; an AREA visual makes it viscerally obvious that you pay for the entire prefix on every turn, and that the bill is the area under the growth line, not its final height. Nothing else in the chapter builds this intuition, and it recurs in the cost worked examples and the interview corner.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The cost arithmetic of a naive agent', immediately after the line 'Naive context accumulation is quadratic in the number of turns.'
new_figures.brief: A ledger-of-turns visual driving home 'you re-send the whole prefix every turn, so the bill is the AREA.' Lay out turns along the horizontal axis (t = 1, 2, 3, …, N) and prompt length up the vertical axis. For each turn draw a horizontal bar whose length is the current prompt L_t = L0 + Σ a_i: turn 1 is a short bar, each later bar a fixed increment a longer, so the stacked bars trace a right triangle. Shade the whole triangle as 'total input tokens billed' and label its area '≈ ½ a N²'. Use tiny illustrative integers only (e.g. a small constant per-turn add such as a = 2 units, L0 tiny) — NO dollar amounts, NO invented benchmark numbers. Overlay or place directly beside it the CAPPED scenario: bars grow for the first few turns then plateau at a fixed length C (a horizontal ceiling line labeled 'cap C'), so the shaded region becomes a small ramp-then-rectangle labeled 'O(N·C)'. A short annotation contrasts the two shaded areas: 'the bill is the AREA under the line — capping trades a triangle for a rectangle.' Animation (enhancement only, must read as a static final frame): sweep turns left→right; on each turn, draw the new bar and tick a running 'tokens billed' counter — the naive counter visibly accelerates (quadratic) while a parallel capped counter climbs linearly and then steadily; final frame shows both full shaded regions side by side with their O(·) labels. Theme-safe: use one accent role for the naive/triangle area and a muted secondary role for the capped/rectangle area; all fills via theme tokens, legible in light and dark. Everything inside the viewBox; axis labels and both O(·) labels must stay legible.

new_figures.name: effective-vs-advertised-window
new_figures.concept: The effective window is a fraction of the advertised window because of two reinforcing effects: U-shaped position accuracy (lost-in-the-middle) and monotonic decay with length (context rot).
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's whole 'Context Rot, Lost-in-the-Middle' section rests on two empirical curves the reader must picture together. A U-curve plus a decay curve, side by side, converts the prose ('capacity, not recommendation') into an instant intuition and directly motivates the actionable rule (place content at edges) and the needle-in-haystack code. It is the most-cited pair of phenomena in the chapter (recurs in Key Takeaways and Interview Corner).
new_figures.placement: Section 'Context Rot, Lost-in-the-Middle & Position Effects', at the top of the section (before the 'Lost-in-the-middle' subheading) so both curves frame the discussion.
new_figures.brief: Two coupled panels sharing one visual language; all curves QUALITATIVE with no numeric accuracy values (invent no benchmark numbers). PANEL A — 'Position effect (lost in the middle)': x-axis = position of the relevant token within the context (labeled start → middle → end), y-axis = retrieval accuracy (label only 'high' at top / 'low' at bottom, no numbers). Draw a clear U-shaped curve: high at both edges, sagging to a trough in the middle. Annotate the left edge 'primacy / attention sink', the right edge 'recency', and the trough 'buried — signal dies here'. Add a small actionable callout banner: 'put critical content at the edges, never the middle.' PANEL B — 'Length effect (context rot)': x-axis = context length (short → long), y-axis = accuracy (high/low, no numbers). Draw a downward-sloping curve. Shade the left region up to a soft threshold as 'effective window (usable)' and the region beyond as 'advertised but degraded'; place an 'advertised max' tick at the far right with a small gap bracket labeled 'you rarely want to be here.' Optionally a tiny schematic inset bridging the panels: a length×depth grid of cells with shading that is bright at the corners/edges and dim in the center-middle, captioned 'needle-in-haystack sweep — measure YOUR effective window' — keep it schematic, no numbers in cells. Theme-safe fills/strokes via tokens; both panels legible in light and dark; keep labels non-overlapping and the reading order left panel → right panel.

new_figures.name: prompt-cache-prefix-stability
new_figures.concept: Prefix caching reuses the prior KV cache up to the first byte that differs; ordering context most-stable→least-stable keeps the hit long, while a volatile token at the top busts everything.
new_figures.rationale: The caching section names 'keep the prefix byte-stable' as 'the one rule' and says the highest-leverage single change to agent economics is prefix stability. The abstract 'longest common prefix / first differing byte' is exactly the kind of thing a good-vs-bad tape diagram makes obvious in one glance, and it directly explains the cache-busting mistakes list and the caching worked example.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The one rule: keep the prefix byte-stable' (Prompt Caching for Agents), replacing/upgrading the ASCII GOOD/BAD sketch.
new_figures.brief: Two stacked horizontal request 'tapes', GOOD on top and BAD below, each a left-to-right row of labeled segment blocks: [system prompt][tool schemas][retrieved — stable][history …][new turn]. GOOD tape: everything from the far left through 'history' is byte-identical to the previous request; shade that whole span in a calm/secondary tone labeled 'cached prefix — reused (≈ 10× cheaper)'. Draw a bold vertical line labeled 'cache breakpoint = first differing byte' just before the final small [new turn] block, which is shaded in the accent tone and labeled 'prefilled fresh (full price)'. BAD tape: insert a small volatile chip at the FAR LEFT, before the system prompt, labeled 'It is 14:32:07 · req-id · turn #'; draw the first-differing-byte line at position ~0 so essentially the entire tape is accent-shaded and labeled 'cache MISS — full price every turn.' Add a one-line rule banner spanning both: 'the cache hit ends at the first byte that differs — move all volatile content to the end.' Include a small legend distinguishing 'cached (discount)' from 'prefilled / miss (full price)'. Optional tiny aside chip near the GOOD tape's history block: 'compaction rewrites the head → busts the cache (compact deliberately).' You may show clearly-illustrative token counts consistent with the chapter's worked example (prefix ≈ 12k, new turn ≈ 1.5k) but mark them as illustrative; no benchmark claims. Theme-safe via tokens; cached vs miss distinguished by tone role not hardcoded hue; all blocks and labels inside the viewBox, no overlap.

new_figures.name: context-window-memory-hierarchy
new_figures.concept: The context window is fast/small/volatile RAM and the file system is slow/large/durable disk; the agent pages relevant state in and writes durable state out, so the plan survives compaction.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter calls this 'the deepest idea in modern context engineering' and frames the whole external-context and scratchpad machinery around the RAM-vs-disk (MemGPT) hierarchy, explicitly echoing the GPU memory-hierarchy chapter. A two-tier diagram showing page-in / page-out and a compaction event that wipes RAM while the plan is re-read from disk makes 'why externalize state' click far better than the prose can.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Structured External Context: Files, Scratchpads & State', right after the opening RAM-vs-disk paragraph and before 'The scratchpad / plan file'.
new_figures.brief: A two-tier memory hierarchy deliberately echoing a GPU RAM/disk diagram. TOP TIER — a visibly SMALL bounded box labeled 'Context window — RAM: fast, small, volatile', holding a few live segment chips: 'pinned plan', 'recent turns', 'current task'. BOTTOM TIER — a visibly WIDE, large box labeled 'External store — disk: slow, large, durable', holding file chips: PLAN.md, TODO.md, source files, 'past-episode memory'. Between the tiers, two labeled bidirectional arrows: an upward arrow 'read relevant state IN (page-in)' and a downward arrow 'write durable state OUT (page-out)'. Now depict a COMPACTION event on the RAM tier: a small broom/erase motif clearing the 'recent turns' / old history chips (label 'compaction wipes conversation history'), paired with a distinct arrow from PLAN.md on disk repopulating the 'pinned plan' chip (label 'plan is re-read from disk each turn → survives compaction, rebuilt from ground truth'). Reinforce scale with a caption: 'window = ephemeral working memory; disk = durable ground truth.' Keep the RAM box clearly smaller than the disk box to convey the capacity gap. Theme-safe via tokens; legible in both themes; reading order top(RAM) → arrows → bottom(disk); no overlapping labels. Static — the labeled arrows convey the paging; motion is not required.

new_figures.name: token-budget-allocator
new_figures.concept: Manage the window like a memory allocator: per-segment budgets, pinned load-bearing items, a global-ceiling backstop, and cost-ordered overflow handling evict → truncate → compact with a breadcrumb left behind.
new_figures.rationale: The Token Budgeting section introduces a whole ContextBuilder/Segment machine (budgets, pinning, eviction breadcrumbs, global ceiling) plus the evict/truncate/compact escalation summarized in the aside and Key Takeaways. A single annotated window bar showing the partition, the lock icons, the overflow-eviction-with-breadcrumb, and the reserved output headroom turns a page of code into one legible mechanism.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Token Budgeting: Allocating the Window', after the budget-partition equation (W_eff = b_sys + b_tools + …) and before 'A concrete budget manager'.
new_figures.brief: A single horizontal bar representing W_eff, partitioned left-to-right into labeled sub-budget cells using the chapter's illustrative 32K split: system+tools ≈ 2K (small, lock icon = pinned), retrieved code ≈ 8K, conversation history ≈ 16K (largest, elastic), scratchpad ≈ 2K, and a reserved output tail ≈ 4K drawn hatched and labeled 'output headroom — keep empty'. Show each segment partly filled versus its cap. Put small lock icons on the pinned pieces (system prompt, current task, active plan). Depict an OVERFLOW event on the history segment: its fill exceeds its cap; an arrow shows the oldest UNPINNED item being dropped and replaced by a small breadcrumb chip '[elided 1,240 tokens]' (illustrative integer). Along the bottom, a cost-ordered escalation strip: 'evict (cheap, no model call) → truncate over-long results → compact (costs a model call)'. Draw a dashed vertical/overlaid line labeled 'global ceiling = W_eff − output_reserve' across the bar; add a small arrow showing the largest elastic segment (history) being squeezed when total exceeds the ceiling. Distinguish pinned vs elastic by outline style / lock icon, NOT by a hardcoded color. Theme-safe via tokens; all cells, icons, and labels inside the viewBox and non-overlapping; reading order bar (left→right) then escalation strip below.

summary: A concept-rich but figure-poor chapter (zero existing figures) with ~8 load-bearing mechanisms. I propose 5 prioritized figures, two of them must-haves. P1: (1) an AREA-based visual of quadratic O(N²) context accumulation vs an O(N·C) cap — the chapter's central quantitative claim; (2) the effective-vs-advertised window, pairing the lost-in-the-middle U-curve with the context-rot length-decay curve. P2: (3) a good/bad prompt-cache prefix-stability tape showing the first-differing-byte cutoff; (4) the RAM-vs-disk memory hierarchy with page-in/out and a compaction event the plan survives. P3: (5) the token-budget allocator bar with per-segment caps, pinning, breadcrumb eviction, and the global ceiling. All briefs are theme-safe, use only small illustrative integers consistent with the chapter, invent no benchmark numbers, and read as static frames (only figure 1 uses animation, as enhancement).
